# Transcrição: 02-The Different Types of Leveraged Buyouts

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Now that you're familiar with some of the deal terms, let's spend the next few lessons going in depth on a few of them. I'm going to start in this lesson by diving into a deeper conversation about Leverage Buyouts or LBOs. Like we covered last lesson, a Leverage Buyout uses the business's assets and current cash flow to raise the money necessary for you to buy it. This means you won't be paying anything upfront. You will, however, be paying for the business in stages once you take it over. Let's look at a quick example. Say you're buying a business for $2 million. You agree to pay $500,000 at closing. This means you'll still owe a million and a half dollars to the seller in future payments. This could take several years. Those future payments will come out of the business's future cash flow. And the $500,000 down payment on the business you made, that will be covered by the assets still in the business. Could be receivables, it could be real equipment, it could be any asset that you can leverage. By leveraged, all we mean is that you borrow money against the value of some assets on the business's balance sheet. Depending on the business you're doing an LBO on and how much you borrow against the balance sheet, you might be able to even leverage more cash than what you need for the down payment for buying the business. You could use the additional cash to cover other closing costs, like professional services such as a lawyer, accountant, or broker. You could reinvest it in the business for future growth, pay yourself a little more as the new owner, or many other things. But that, in essence, is how a typical leverage buyout works. The details can change depending on the specifics. How much you want to leverage the business, for what assets you want to lever, and how much the seller is willing to let you do that, it all can be different depending on each and every deal. Depending on what kind of business you're buying, the details can change entirely. Even the same kind of business as it can change, it's all about the negotiation. And in addition to that, it's very unlikely that the first few deals you look at, you're going to come across a sweetheart LBO structure. That's why you need to look at a lot of different deals. Now, just because it's hard to find an LBO opportunity, that does not mean it's a bad deal. In fact, there are a lot of them out there, you just need to know what you're looking for and learn to negotiate them. It does mean that you're going to have to search for the right business candidate to do this type of deal with. So look, as you're searching for a deal, if you find a great business that's not an LBO, especially in a market that you have some expertise in, don't worry. You shouldn't let that stop you from doing another deal type that we covered in the previous lesson. Creative deal making is an art.

## [03:00 - 06:04]

So as long as you go through this course, pay attention to the details and have an open mind and talk to a lot of people about deals, you will find the right deal for you. And if by chance you're acquiring a large business, which I obviously don't recommend targeting as your first deal, there are a lot of other partners you can bring to get the cash, like venture capital, private equity, or high net worth investors. But you'll need to give up a significant amount of equity potentially. We'll have resources for you later on about these types of firms or these kind of deals. We'll mostly be talking about them in the exit stage of your business, but you can also leverage that information for funding if you happen to be acquiring a much larger business. We're going to cover all the different type of financing options available to you a little later on. So if you're getting nervous about a hypothetical example where you're talking about needing a six figure down payment, don't worry, there are a lot of different ways to skin this cap. The next type of leverage buyout to know is called a management buyout. This is a situation where you didn't start the business. You're effectively a group of intrapreneurs and your key employees or contributors and you want to buy the business from a retiring owner. If you are not an employee and you were brought into the business, it would be called a management buy-in. There is also a combination of two strategies called a buy-in management buyout. So let's just say that you're working in a business, you know a owner wants to sell, you go find the money with somebody who also understands the business and collectively you buy out the owner. The next type of LBO I want to cover is the bolt-on acquisition. That's where you're acquiring and adding on a second, third, or fourth, or fifth. It doesn't matter the number of locations, but you're adding a business to your business that complements it. Maybe you offer marketing services to e-com retailers and you go out and you buy a bolt-on customer data software company that optimizes your core marketing services, but it's something else you can charge for. When you combine the two businesses successfully, your cost should go down and your revenue and margin should go up. Come time to exit your business. It will also help you get a higher multiple on your valuation because you earn more and do more. Okay, so that's a bolt-on acquisition. Now let's talk about consulting in exchange for equity in the business, then using an LBO to acquire it all together. In this case, you're bringing your expertise and insights to a business to help it grow.
